schema_json.description:
LLM contract for the agentic search loop 'Reason' step.

After each round of searches, the LLM analyzes results and decides
whether to continue searching or converge. Generates targeted
follow-up queries informed by what was found so far.

key_findings.description: 3-5 key findings from the latest round
key_findings.title: Key Findings

perspective_gaps.description: Underrepresented STORM perspectives in current results
perspective_gaps.title: Perspective Gaps

web_queries.description: 3-6 web queries for the next round
web_queries.title: Web Queries

academic_queries.description: 1-3 Semantic Scholar queries using precise terminology
academic_queries.title: Academic Queries

exa_queries.description: 0-1 semantic queries for Exa neural search
exa_queries.title: Exa Queries

should_continue.description: Whether another search round is needed
should_continue.title: Should Continue

reasoning.description: Brief rationale for the convergence assessment
reasoning.title: Reasoning

knowledge_gaps.description: Specific knowledge gaps identified from reading content
knowledge_gaps.title: Knowledge Gaps

schema_json.title: AgenticRoundAnalysis

prompt:
You are a research search strategist conducting an agentic deep research loop.

RESEARCH QUESTION: How has the EU Digital Services Act (Regulation 2022/2065) changed large platform moderation obligations since February 2024?

ROUND HISTORY:
Round 1: 9 queries -> 79 web + 30 academic, 51 new URLs, 6 pages read

KNOWLEDGE ACQUIRED (6 pages read so far):
[Page 1] A Reflection on Regulation 2022/2065 (Digital Services Act—DSA ...
  Summary: Abstract The European Union made digital transformation one of its priorities to be achieved by 2024, through the objective of a “Europe fit for the digital age. Empowering people through a new generation of technologies”. Most EU citizens now use such services in their daily lives.
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Abstract The European Union made digital transformation one of its priorities to be achieved by 2024, through the objective of a “Europe fit for the digital age.; For this reason, the European Parliament adopted a few months ago, Regulation 2022/2065 of 19/10/2022 on the single market for digital services.; Finally, the authors will discuss the 2030 Agenda and the Digital Services Regulation from a Portuguese and Spanish perspective, seeking to identify whether the regulation has considered the Agenda’s sustainable development goals.
[Page 2] The impact of the Digital Services Act on digital platforms
  Summary: With the Digital Services Act (DSA) platforms have already changed their systems and interfaces to provide a safer online experience for all Europeans. The DSA significantly improves the mechanisms for the removal of illegal content and for the effective protection of users’ fundamental rights online, including freedom of speech. It also creates a stronger public oversight mechanism for online platforms, particularly for Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) and Search Engines (VLOSEs).
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: These have over 45 million monthly users, reaching more than 10% of the EU’s population.; Easier reporting of illegal content The DSA requires platforms to put in place measures to counter the spreading of illegal goods, services or content online, such as mechanisms for users to flag such content.; Greater transparency in content moderation and more options to appeal Online platforms are a digital space where we express ourselves, showcase our work and connect with friends or customers.
[Page 3] EU Digital Regulation as Industry Shaping Policy: The DSA ...
  Summary: The Network Law Review is pleased to present a special issue on “Industrial Policy and Competitiveness,” prepared in collaboration with the International Center for Law & Economics (ICLE). This issue gathers leading scholars to explore a central question: What are the boundaries between competition and industrial policy? ** Abstract: The European Union’s recent wave of digital regulations, especially the Digital Services Act (DSA), highlights a strategic use of law as an instrument of industrial
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Understanding whether the DSA enables or constrains innovation will be central to assessing its long-term policy impact.; Content moderation balances freedom of speech rights and provides legal reasons for moderation decisions, divided in legal reasons (e.g., national criminal law) or contractual reasons (Terms of Service).; The ToS automatically implement the platform’s Community Standards in the contract between the user and the platform to define what is allowed or restricted on the platform.
[Page 4] Article 22 Digital Services Act: Building trust with trusted flaggers
  Summary: Article 22 Digital Services Act: Building trust with trusted flaggers Abstract Trusted flaggers have long played a role in content moderation: a bilateral, voluntary affair between online platforms and individuals or organisations that are afforded prioritised access to the content moderation process. Due to their expertise, they are trusted to ‘flag’ illegal or harmful content. Article 22 Digital Services Act formalises this framework, allowing governmental and non-governmental organisations to
  Perspectives: Scientific
  Key facts: Article 22 Digital Services Act: Building trust with trusted flaggers Abstract Trusted flaggers have long played a role in content moderation: a bilateral, voluntary affair between online platforms and individuals or organisations that are afforded p; Due to their expertise, they are trusted to ‘flag’ illegal or harmful content.; Introduction The selection of the first trusted flaggers under Article 22 Digital Services Act (Regulation 2022/2065, ‘DSA’) has sparked public debate, fuelled by concerns over their power and potential impact on freedom of expression on the internet
[Page 5] No. 85: The EU's Digital Services Act and Its Impact on Online ...
  Summary: Abstract On the 23rd of April 2022, the European Parliament and the Member States of the EU came to a political consensus regarding the proposal for the Digital Services Act (DSA), initially put forth by the European Commission in December 2020. Subsequently, this proposal received endorsement on the 5th of July 2022. On the 4th of October 2022, the Council of the European Union provided its definitive consent to the DSA Regulation.

  Covered (4/9): Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic
  Uncovered: Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends

Based on what has been READ:
1. Decompose the research question into 5-8 sub-questions.
   Which sub-questions have ZERO or WEAK evidence?
   Which sub-questions are well-answered with strong evidence?
2. What CONTRADICTIONS exist between sources? Flag conflicting evidence.
3. What specific KNOWLEDGE GAPS remain? List them as specific factual questions
   we CANNOT yet answer. 'none' is almost never correct for a complex research question.
4. Rate overall confidence in accumulated knowledge: LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH with reasoning.
5. Which perspectives need more coverage?
6. Generate 3-6 targeted WEB queries to fill those gaps.
7. Generate 1-3 ACADEMIC queries using precise terminology.
8. Optionally generate 0-1 semantic EXA queries.
9. Assess ANSWER COMPLETENESS for the research question:
   - List specific sub-questions that remain UNANSWERED.
   - For each answered sub-question, rate evidence strength (weak/moderate/strong).
   - 'expanding': Major sub-questions still unanswered.
   - 'narrowing': Most sub-questions answered, seeking confirmation/depth.
   - 'saturated': All sub-questions answered with strong evidence.
10. Should we continue searching?

Focus on queries that address content-level gaps, not just missing URLs.
